🤖 *LabelOps — Conflict Resolution (escalation).*

**CI:** ✅ All 50 checks passing (last run: Apr 21).

**Conflicts:** 8 modify/delete conflicts in IL baseline files — `main` deleted the old split `*.debug.bsl`/`*.release.bsl` files (PR #19611), while this PR modified them. This has been correctly diagnosed in my last two runs, but the merge commit is too large for the push API (main is 58 commits / 478 files / ~170K lines ahead → ~9.8 MB patch vs 1024 KB limit).

**Manual merge needed — here are the steps:**
```bash
git checkout codegen/fix-exception-serialization
git merge origin/main
# Resolve all 8 conflicts by accepting the deletions:
git rm tests/FSharp.Compiler.ComponentTests/EmittedIL/SerializableAttribute/ToplevelModule.fs.RealInternalSignatureOff.il.net472.debug.bsl
git rm tests/FSharp.Compiler.ComponentTests/EmittedIL/SerializableAttribute/ToplevelModule.fs.RealInternalSignatureOff.il.netcore.release.bsl
git rm tests/FSharp.Compiler.ComponentTests/EmittedIL/SerializableAttribute/ToplevelModule.fs.RealInternalSignatureOn.il.net472.debug.bsl
git rm tests/FSharp.Compiler.ComponentTests/EmittedIL/SerializableAttribute/ToplevelModule.fs.RealInternalSignatureOn.il.netcore.release.bsl
git rm tests/FSharp.Compiler.ComponentTests/EmittedIL/SerializableAttribute/ToplevelNamespace.fs.RealInternalSignatureOff.il.net472.debug.bsl
git rm tests/FSharp.Compiler.ComponentTests/EmittedIL/SerializableAttribute/ToplevelNamespace.fs.RealInternalSignatureOff.il.netcore.release.bsl
git rm tests/FSharp.Compiler.ComponentTests/EmittedIL/SerializableAttribute/ToplevelNamespace.fs.RealInternalSignatureOn.il.net472.debug.bsl
git rm tests/FSharp.Compiler.ComponentTests/EmittedIL/SerializableAttribute/ToplevelNamespace.fs.RealInternalSignatureOn.il.netcore.release.bsl
# Then regenerate the unified baselines:
TEST_UPDATE_BSL=1 dotnet test tests/FSharp.Compiler.ComponentTests -c Release --filter "Toplevel"
git add tests/FSharp.Compiler.ComponentTests/EmittedIL/SerializableAttribute/*.netcore.bsl
git merge --continue
git push
```

The old split files (`*.debug.bsl`, `*.release.bsl`) were unified into single `*.bsl` files by PR #19611. Git's rename tracking already auto-merged the serialization changes into the unified `*.net472.bsl` files. Only the `*.netcore.bsl` files need baseline regeneration.

Agent job [25296521601](https://github.com/dotnet/fsharp/actions/runs/25296521601) failed.


**🔒 Lock File Out of Sync**: The workflow could not start because its compiled lock file no longer matches the source markdown.

This means the workflow's `.md` file was edited but `gh aw compile` was not run afterwards to regenerate the corresponding `.lock.yml` file. The agent is prevented from running against a stale configuration to avoid unexpected behaviour.

**To fix**, recompile the workflow:

```bash
gh aw compile
```

Then commit and push the updated `.lock.yml` file.

<details>
<summary>More ways to recompile</summary>

**Using the gh-aw MCP server** (if configured):

```json
{ "tool": "compile", "arguments": { "validate": true } }
```

**Recompile all workflows at once:**

```bash
gh aw compile --all
```

**Verify the result:**

```bash
gh aw compile --validate
```

</details>

<details>
<summary>How to investigate the mismatch</summary>

The workflow run logs contain a verbose debug pass that shows exactly what was hashed. Search the **Check workflow lock file** step logs for lines starting with `[hash-debug]` to see:

- The raw frontmatter text that was used as input
- Any imported files that were included in the hash
- The canonical JSON that was fed to SHA-256
- The resulting hash value

This makes it easy to spot accidental whitespace changes, encoding differences, or import path drift.

</details>

<details>
<summary>How to disable this check</summary>

> [!CAUTION]
> Disabling this check means the agent can run against an out-of-date compiled workflow. Only disable it if you have an alternative mechanism to keep lock files in sync.

Set `stale-check: false` in the `on:` section of your workflow frontmatter:

```yaml
on:
  issues:
    types: [opened]
  stale-check: false
```

After editing, recompile the workflow: `gh aw compile`

</details>

This means the workflow's `.md` file was edited but `gh aw compile` was not run afterwards to regenerate the corresponding `.lock.yml` file. The agent is prevented from running against a stale configuration to avoid unexpected behaviour.

**To fix**, recompile the workflow:

```bash
gh aw compile
```

Then commit and push the updated `.lock.yml` file.

<details>
<summary>More ways to recompile</summary>

**Using the gh-aw MCP server** (if configured):

```json
{ "tool": "compile", "arguments": { "validate": true } }
```

**Recompile all workflows at once:**

```bash
gh aw compile --all
```

**Verify the result:**

```bash
gh aw compile --validate
```

</details>

<details>
<summary>How to investigate the mismatch</summary>

The workflow run logs contain a verbose debug pass that shows exactly what was hashed. Search the **Check workflow lock file** step logs for lines starting with `[hash-debug]` to see:

- The raw frontmatter text that was used as input
- Any imported files that were included in the hash
- The canonical JSON that was fed to SHA-256
- The resulting hash value

This makes it easy to spot accidental whitespace changes, encoding differences, or import path drift.

</details>

<details>
<summary>How to disable this check</summary>

> [!CAUTION]
> Disabling this check means the agent can run against an out-of-date compiled workflow. Only disable it if you have an alternative mechanism to keep lock files in sync.

Set `stale-check: false` in the `on:` section of your workflow frontmatter:

```yaml
on:
  issues:
    types: [opened]
  stale-check: false
```

After editing, recompile the workflow: `gh aw compile`

</details>

🤖 *This is an automated response from Repo Assist.*

Confirmed **still present** in **F# 11.0** (FSI 15.2.101.0, commit `2535da2`, 2026-04-20).

**Verified reproduction** — Debug raises the exception, Release silently skips it:

```fsharp
type SomeOutputType() =
    member x.End = ()

let someFunctionWithReturnType () = 
    failwith "This should be raised"
    SomeOutputType()

let theTaskAtHand () = 
    task {
        (someFunctionWithReturnType ()).End
    }

try
    theTaskAtHand().Wait()
    printfn "BUG: Exception was NOT raised!"
with
| :? System.AggregateException as e ->
    printfn "PASS: Exception raised: %s" (e.InnerException.Message)
```

| Mode | Output |
|------|--------|
| `dotnet run -c Debug` | `PASS: Exception raised: This should be raised` ✅ |
| `dotnet run -c Release` | `BUG: Exception was NOT raised!` ❌ |

**Root cause**: The optimizer eliminates the call to `someFunctionWithReturnType()` because the only use of the result is accessing `.End` (a unit-valued property). Since `.End` is a constant `()`, the optimizer treats the entire expression as dead code — but `someFunctionWithReturnType()` has side effects (the `failwith`). This is a correctness bug in the optimizer's side-effect analysis for method calls whose results flow only into unit-typed property access.

This has been reproducible since .NET 6 (original report, 2022) through the current .NET 10 / F# 11.0.

**This issue appears to be fixed** in the current F# compiler (F# 11.0, FSI 15.2.101.0, commit `76853e03b`).

### Verification

I reproduced the exact scenario from the issue body using `--warnon:1182 --warnaserror:1182` (which promotes the unused-open warning to an error). With the current compiler, the build **succeeds** — meaning `open Builder` is correctly recognized as used.

**Test code:**
````fsharp
module Test11626

open System.Threading.Tasks

module Builder =
    type AsyncBuilder with
        member _.Bind (task : Task<'a>, cont : 'a -> Async<'b>) =
            async.Bind (Async.AwaitTask task, cont)

open Builder

let task = Task.FromResult 1
let x = async {
    let! i = task
    return i + 1
}
```

**Build command:**
```
dotnet build --warnon:1182 --warnaserror:1182  →  Build succeeded.
````

**Without `open Builder`**, the code correctly fails with `FS0001: This expression was expected to have type 'Async<'a>' but here has type 'Task<int>'` — confirming the open IS needed and IS now correctly tracked.

The extension method `Bind` on `AsyncBuilder` used in `let!` within the computation expression is now properly accounted for in the unused-opens analysis.

**Verified** on F# 11.0 (FSI 15.2.101.0, commit `c3c01c99`, 2026-04-15).

**Existing test coverage** — comprehensive regression tests were added by the same PR:

- [`CompletionTests.fs#L727-L736`](https://github.com/dotnet/fsharp/blob/c3c01c991d17643700d343cee5c5a1e20c06ce03/tests/FSharp.Compiler.Service.Tests/CompletionTests.fs#L727-L736): C# obsolete field is hidden
- [`CompletionTests.fs#L738-L747`](https://github.com/dotnet/fsharp/blob/c3c01c991d17643700d343cee5c5a1e20c06ce03/tests/FSharp.Compiler.Service.Tests/CompletionTests.fs#L738-L747): C# obsolete method is hidden
- [`CompletionTests.fs#L749-L758`](https://github.com/dotnet/fsharp/blob/c3c01c991d17643700d343cee5c5a1e20c06ce03/tests/FSharp.Compiler.Service.Tests/CompletionTests.fs#L749-L758): C# obsolete property is hidden
- [`CompletionTests.fs#L760-L769`](https://github.com/dotnet/fsharp/blob/c3c01c991d17643700d343cee5c5a1e20c06ce03/tests/FSharp.Compiler.Service.Tests/CompletionTests.fs#L760-L769): C# obsolete event is hidden
- [`CompletionTests.fs#L771-L783`](https://github.com/dotnet/fsharp/blob/c3c01c991d17643700d343cee5c5a1e20c06ce03/tests/FSharp.Compiler.Service.Tests/CompletionTests.fs#L771-L783): Non-obsolete members always shown

This issue is **fixed** in the current `main` (F# 11.0, FSI 15.2.101.0, commit `c3c01c99`, 2026-04-15).

**Verification:**
````fsharp
for i in 1 .. 10 do
    printfn ""
    printfn "" |> ignore
    123
```

**Result** (with `--test:ErrorRanges`):
```
(4,5-4,8): warning FS0020: The result of this expression has type 'int' and is implicitly ignored...
````

The warning range is now `(4,5-4,8)` — pointing to just `123` — matching the expected behavior described in this issue. Previously the range spanned from `printfn ""` through `123`.

Fixed by PR #19504 (merged 2026-04-11), which corrected warning 20 ranges in sequential expressions.

**Existing test coverage:**
- [`WarnExpressionTests.fs#L179-L193`](https://github.com/dotnet/fsharp/blob/c3c01c991d17643700d343cee5c5a1e20c06ce03/tests/FSharp.Compiler.ComponentTests/ErrorMessages/WarnExpressionTests.fs#L179-L193): Exact repro from this issue — `for i in 1..10 do` with intermediate expressions and trailing `123` — asserts warning points to only `123` (Line 7, Col 5-8).

**Confirmed still reproducing** on `d507366` (2026-04-01), F# 11.0, FSharp.Core 11.0.100:

````fsharp
type ResolvingErrorReport = delegate of int * System.Int32 * System.Int32 * int -> unit
```

FCS classification output (sorted by position):
```
Col  5-25  Type=Delegate   Text='ResolvingErrorReport'
Col 28-87  Type=Method     Text='delegate of int * System.Int32 * System.Int32 * int -> unit'
Col 46-58  Type=ValueType  Text='System.Int32'
Col 61-73  Type=ValueType  Text='System.Int32'
````

The entire delegate signature (col 28–87) is classified as `Method`. The fully-qualified `System.Int32` references get their own `ValueType` classification that overrides the `Method` range — but bare `int` keywords do **not** get separate `ValueType` classification. This causes `int` and `System.Int32` to render in different colors despite being the same type, exactly as shown in the issue screenshots.

`@auduchinok`'s [hypothesis](https://github.com/dotnet/fsharp/issues/16982#issuecomment-2034280462) about synthetic members from the delegate representation seems correct — the `Invoke` method generated for the delegate type is what produces the `Method` classification spanning the whole signature.

🤖 *This is an automated response from Regression PR Shepherd.*

This regression test proves the bug in #16154 still exists on Desktop .NET Framework. Closing this PR is recommended.

**The bug**: When a `task { }` computation expression contains branches returning `Some filter` where `filter` is an `IQueryable<T> -> IQueryable<T>` function, the compiler infers the return type as `#IQueryable -> IQueryable` (with a flexible type/implicit SRTP constraint). When combining two such results, the state machine generates an implicit upcast to `obj`. On .NET Framework 4.8, this causes a runtime `VerificationException: Method returnFilter: type argument 'System.Object' violates the constraint of type parameter 'a'`. The workaround is to add explicit type annotations to force `IQueryable<Shape> -> IQueryable<Shape>` (no `#`).

**The test**: [`tests/FSharp.Compiler.ComponentTests/Language/StateMachineTests.fs`](https://github.com/dotnet/fsharp/blob/regression-test/issue16154-75bc3271f0f7f428/tests/FSharp.Compiler.ComponentTests/Language/StateMachineTests.fs) — `Issue 16154 - task CE with IQueryable filter functions should compile and run without VerificationException`. It uses `compileExeAndRun |> shouldSucceed` with the exact repro from the issue report.

**The failure**: `Build WindowsNoRealsig_testDesktop` and `Build WindowsCompressedMetadata_Desktop Batch2` fail on .NET Framework. The Repo Assist bot that labeled this as fixed only tested on Linux/.NET 10 (CoreCLR), where the issue does not reproduce. The VerificationException is specific to the .NET Framework runtime's strict IL verification.

The `AI-thinks-issue-fixed` label has been removed from #16154.

cc `@T-Gro` `@abonie`

🤖 *This is an automated response from Regression PR Shepherd.*

This regression test proves the bug in #12796 still exists on Desktop .NET Framework. Closing this PR is recommended.

**The bug**: `[<DefaultValue([||] : A[])>]` on a record field of type `A[]` triggers an internal compiler error `FS0192: encodeCustomAttrElemType: unrecognized custom element type`. The issue was opened in 2022, and was labeled `AI-thinks-issue-fixed` after the bot detected `SynPat.Record` and `SynPat.QuoteExpr` fixes — but that was a different issue; this bug is specifically about attribute encoding for array-typed `DefaultValue` arguments.

**The test**: [`tests/FSharp.Compiler.ComponentTests/Language/AttributeCheckingTests.fs`](https://github.com/dotnet/fsharp/blob/regression-test/issue12796/tests/FSharp.Compiler.ComponentTests/Language/AttributeCheckingTests.fs) typechecks `type B = { [<DefaultValue([||] : A[])>] BField: A[] }` and expects success.

**The failure**: `Build WindowsNoRealsig_testDesktop` fails — the test fails only on Desktop .NET Framework, not on CoreCLR/Linux. The `FS0192` error is triggered during attribute argument encoding, which happens during type checking on Desktop.

The `AI-thinks-issue-fixed` label has been removed from #12796.

This bug is **still present** in F# 10.0 (FCS **15.2.201.0**, SDK 10.0.201).

**Verification** — calling `FSharpCheckFileResults.GenerateSignature()` on the repro code produces:

````fsharp
namespace FSInteractive
  
  type LocalReadWriteLockCookie =
    interface System.IDisposable
    
    new: locker: obj -> LocalReadWriteLockCookie
```

The generated signature is missing **both** `[<Struct>]` and `[<NoComparison>]`. This is worse than what the issue describes — even the `[<Struct>]` attribute is dropped.

**Consequence** — compiling the implementation against the generated `.fsi` file fails with:
```
error FS0293: The type definitions for type 'LocalReadWriteLockCookie' in the signature
and implementation are not compatible because the signature requires that the type supports
the interface IComparable<LocalReadWriteLockCookie> but the interface has not been implemented
````

The fix would need `GenerateSignature` to emit `[<Struct>]` for struct types and to include the implicit `[<NoComparison; NoEquality>]` attributes that structs with a reference-type field carry.

This bug is **still present** in F# 10.0 (fsc/fsi **15.2.201.0**, SDK 10.0.201, Linux/net10.0).

**Minimal repro** (self-contained, no VS required):

````fsharp
module Test

let mutable i = 0

type T() =
    member x.indexed1
        with get (a1: obj) = i <- i + 1; 1
        and  set (a1: obj) (value: int) = i <- i + 1

module Extensions =
    type T with
        member x.indexed1
            with get (aa1: obj) = i <- i + 1; 1
            and  set (aa1: obj) (value: int) = i <- i + 1

let t = T()
open Extensions
t.indexed1(aa1 = "nok") <- 1   // FS0193 here
```

**Error:**
```
error FS0193: internal error: The input must be non-negative. (Parameter 'n')
````

The root cause was already identified in the first comment: the indexer setter with a named argument hits wrong array indexing in `MethodCalls.fs` around the `calledArgs` list (line ~581). The fix would need to account for the fact that a named argument (`aa1`) can precede the unnamed `value` argument, so the indexing into the positional args list goes negative.

🤖 *This is an automated response from Repo Assist.*

This bug is **still present** in F# 10.0 (fsc **15.2.201.0**, SDK 10.0.201, Linux/net10.0).

**Exact repro:**

````fsharp
open System.Numerics

type Test<'T when 'T :> INumber<'T>> = 
    { One: 'T; Two: 'T; Three: 'T } with
    member this.ThreeSum = this.One + this.Two + this.Three
```

**Error:**
```
error FS0670: This code is not sufficiently generic. The type variable ^T when ^T :> INumber<^T>
could not be generalized because it would escape its scope.
````

The `+` operator on `INumber<'T>` is a static abstract member, so it goes through SRTP. The first `this.One + this.Two` produces an intermediate SRTP call result whose type variable is constrained to `^T`. When the second `+ this.Three` is applied, the type variable for the intermediate result can no longer be generalised — the two chained `+` calls create a type variable that "escapes" the member's scope.

The workaround (using an intermediate `let` binding or chaining via `'T.op_CheckedAddition(...)`) avoids the issue but shouldn't be necessary.

This issue appears to be **fixed** in the current F# 11.0 development build.

**Verification** — tested on commit `a0cce49` (dotnet/fsharp `main`, 2026-03-30) using **F# Interactive 15.2.100.0 for F# 11.0**:

````fsharp
#r "nuget: FSharpPlus, 1.6.1"
open FSharpPlus

module Probability =
    type Prob<'t> = Prob of List<'t * float>
    type Prob<'a> with
        static member probMap f (Prob prob : Prob<'a>) : Prob<'b> = List.map (fun (x, p) -> (f x, p)) prob |> Prob
        static member flatten (Prob xs : Prob<Prob<'a>>) : Prob<'a> =
            let multAll (Prob innerxs, p) = List.map (fun (x, r) -> (x, p * r)) innerxs
            Prob (List.map multAll xs |> List.concat)
        static member Map (prob:Prob<'a>, f) = Prob.probMap f prob : Prob<'b>
        static member Return (x) = Prob [(x, 1.0)] : Prob<'a>
        static member (>>=) (prob:Prob<'a>, f:'a -> Prob<'b>) = Prob.flatten (map f prob) : Prob<'b>

open Probability

let res120 = map (fun x -> -x) (Prob [(3, 0.5); (5, 0.25); (9, 0.25)])

type Coin = Heads | Tails
let coin = Prob [(Heads, 0.5); (Tails, 0.5)]
let loadedCoin = Prob [(Heads, 0.1); (Tails, 0.9)]

let flipThree : Prob<bool> =
    monad {
        let! a = coin
        let! b = coin
        let! c = loadedCoin
        return [a;b;c] |> List.forall (fun x -> x = Tails)
    }
```

**Output:**
```
res120: Prob [(-3, 0.5); (-5, 0.25); (-9, 0.25)]
flipThree: Prob [(false, 0.025); (false, 0.225); (false, 0.025); (false, 0.225);
                 (false, 0.025); (false, 0.225); (false, 0.025); (true, 0.225)]
````

open System.Linq
type Shape = {x: int; y: int}

let returnFilter condition =
    task {
        if condition = "a" then
            let filter1 : IQueryable<Shape> -> IQueryable<Shape> =
                fun data -> data.Where(fun a -> a.x = 1)
            return Some filter1
        elif condition = "b" then
            let filter2 : IQueryable<Shape> -> IQueryable<Shape> =
                fun data -> data.Where(fun a -> a.y <> 2)
            return Some filter2
        else
            return None
    }

let data = [{x = 1; y = 1}; {x = 2; y = 2}].AsQueryable()
let result = 
    task {
        let! f1 = returnFilter "a"
        let! f2 = returnFilter "b"
        match f1, f2 with
        | Some filter1, Some filter2 ->
            return data |> filter2 |> filter1 |> Seq.toList
        | _ -> return []
    } |> (fun t -> t.GetAwaiter().GetResult())

```

Verified still present in **F# 11.0** (bootstrap fsi v15.2.100.0, commit `a0cce49`, 2026-03-27) and in the system **F# 10.0** (v14.0.105.0).

**Debug mode**: ✅ prints `ok`  
**Release mode**: ❌ throws `TypeLoadException`  
**FSI** (F# 11.0): ❌ throws `FS0193 internal error: Unable to load one or more of the requested types`

````fsharp
// Self-contained repro (no Ref<> — uses cell.Value)
let inline refEquals<'a when 'a : not struct> (a : 'a) (b : 'a) = obj.ReferenceEquals (a, b)

let inline tee f x =
    f x
    x

let memoizeLatestRef (f: 'a -> 'b) =
    let cell = ref None
    let f' (x: 'a) =
        match cell.Value with
        | Some (x', value) when refEquals x' x -> value
        | _ -> f x |> tee (fun y -> cell.Value <- Some (x, y))
    f'

[<EntryPoint>]
let main _ =
    let f: string -> string = memoizeLatestRef id
    printfn "%s" (f "ok") // Release: System.TypeLoadException
    0
```

```
Unhandled exception. System.TypeLoadException: Method 'Specialize' on type
'memoizeLatestRef@5-1' tried to implicitly override a method with weaker
type parameter constraints.
````

The `Specialize` mention confirms this is a codegen issue where the optimizer is failing to properly inline across a specialization boundary — the generic constraints on the closure type don't match the interface it is trying to implement after optimization. Debug mode disables inlining and avoids the problem.

🤖 *This is an automated response from Repo Assist.*

Confirmed still present on latest main as of commit **a0cce49** (F# 11.0 / FSI `15.2.100.0`), tested on Linux (.NET 10.0).

**Reproduction** (both Debug and Release, `dotnet run`):

````fsharp
type IFoo<'T when 'T : unmanaged> =
    abstract member Pointer : nativeptr<'T>

type Broken() =
    member x.Pointer : nativeptr<int> = Unchecked.defaultof<_>
    interface IFoo<int> with
        member x.Pointer = x.Pointer

type Working<'T when 'T : unmanaged>() =
    member x.Pointer : nativeptr<'T> = Unchecked.defaultof<_>
    interface IFoo<'T> with
        member x.Pointer = x.Pointer

[<EntryPoint>]
let main _ =
    let _a = Working<int>()
    let _b = Broken()   // <-- throws here
    0
```

**Output:**
```
Unhandled exception. System.TypeLoadException: Signature of the body and declaration in a method implementation do not match.
  Type: 'Broken'.
```

Also reproducible via `dotnet fsi`:
```
error FS0193: internal error: Unable to load one or more of the requested types.
Signature of the body and declaration in a method implementation do not match. Type: 'Broken'.
````

**Analysis:** The `Broken` type is non-generic but implements a concrete instantiation of a generic interface (`IFoo<int>`). When the interface member involves `nativeptr<_>`, the compiler emits an explicit interface override whose signature doesn't satisfy the CLR's type-identity requirements for `nativeptr<int>` (which is `int32*` in IL). The `Working<'T>` variant avoids this because its type parameter is preserved through to the interface. No existing test covers this scenario.

The compiler produces an opaque internal IL error instead of a user-friendly diagnostic when `[]` is applied to a tuple destructuring binding. **Reproduction (FSI):** ````fsharp [] let a, b = 1, 2 ``` **Output:** ``` error FS0192: internal error: Error in pass2 for type FSI_0001, error: duplicate entry 'A@' in field table ```` **Reproduction (project build):** ````fsharp module M [] let a, b = 1, 2 ``` **Output:** ``` FSC : error FS2014: A problem occurred writing the binary '...': Error in pass2 for type M, error: duplicate entry 'get_A' in method table ```` The `CompiledName` attribute is silently accepted by the type checker (no error or warning), and the problem is only discovered in the IL writing phase — far from the source location. The fix should be a proper diagnostic during type checking: applying `[]` to a tuple or pattern binding that defines multiple names should be an error (e.g. FS3500-range or similar), since there is no single name to rename. > Generated by [Repo Assist](https://github.com/dotnet/fsharp/actions/runs/23634849828) > [!NOTE] >

🤖 *This is an automated response from Repo Assist.* Confirmed still present in **F# 10.0 / FSI 14.0.105.0** (commit `a21c12f`, dotnet/fsharp `main`, 2026-03-27). **Verification:** The global default cancellation token is passed directly to async computations — no per-run wrapper is created: ```fsharp let getToken() = let mutable tok = Unchecked.defaultof async { let! ct = Async.CancellationToken in tok <- ct } |> Async.RunSynchronously tok let t1 = getToken() let t2 = getToken() printfn "Same global token each run: %b" (t1.Equals(t2)) // true printfn "Is Async.DefaultCancellationToken: %b" (t1.Equals(Async.DefaultCancellationToken)) // true ``` In `async.fs`, `RunSynchronously` passes `defaultCancellationTokenSource.Token` directly when no user token is given (line ~1510). Any linked `CancellationTokenSource` created inside an async computation (e.g. `CancellationTokenSource.CreateLinkedTokenSource ct`) will register a callback on this global token. If the linked CTS is not disposed, that callback remains registered indefinitely. **Proposed fix** (from the original discussion, still applicable): wrap `defaultCancellationTokenSource.Token` in a per-run linked `CancellationTokenSource` inside `RunSynchronously`/`Start`/etc., and dispose it when the computation completes. This ensures linked CTS registrations are cleaned up automatically after each run. > [!NOTE] >

🤖 *This is an automated response from Repo Assist.* Confirmed still present in **F# 10.0 / FSI 14.0.105.0** (commit `a21c12f`, dotnet/fsharp `main`, 2026-03-27). **Repro (verified):** ```fsharp let mutable count = 0 let resource = { new System.IDisposable with member x.Dispose() = count <- count + 1 } do use a as b = resource in () printfn "Dispose count (expected 1): %d" count // prints 2 let mutable count2 = 0 let resource2 = { new System.IDisposable with member x.Dispose() = count2 <- count2 + 1 } do use a as b as c = resource2 in () printfn "Dispose count (expected 1): %d" count2 // prints 3 ``` Pattern: `N` `as`-bindings → `N+1` dispose calls. --- **Root cause** (in `CheckExpressions.fs`, `TcLetBinding`): The `mkCleanup` function iterates `allValsDefinedByPattern` and generates a `BuildDisposableCleanup` for **every** variable defined by the pattern: ```fsharp // mkCleanup in TcLetBinding (allValsDefinedByPattern, ...) ||> List.foldBack (fun v ... -> let cleanupE = BuildDisposableCleanup cenv env m v mkTryFinally g (bodyExpr, cleanupE, ...) ) ``` For `use a as b = resource`, `allValsDefinedByPattern = [a; b]`, so `Dispose()` is called on both `a` and `b` — the same underlying object — resulting in 2 calls. For `use x = resource` (no `as`), `allValsDefinedByPattern = [x]` and `x` is `patternInputTmp`, so it disposes once correctly. **Fix**: In `mkCleanup`, only dispose `patternInputTmp` (the single variable holding the actual resource), not all alias-bindings. The type-constraint check (`system_IDisposableNull_ty`) should be applied to `patternInputTmp` only. > [!NOTE] >

🤖 *This is an automated response from Repo Assist.* Tested in **F# 10.0** (FSI 14.0.105.0, commit `880dda8` of dotnet/fsharp). The inconsistency is still present. **Current behavior:** ```fsharp // {||} as a VALUE — works fine > let x = {||};; val x : {| |} = { } // {||} as a TYPE ANNOTATION — fails > let y : {||} = {||};; let y : {||} = {||};; ----------^^ /home/runner/work/fsharp/fsharp/stdin(1,11): error FS0010: Unexpected symbol '|}' in field declaration. Expected identifier or other token. ``` **Status:** `{||}` remains a valid expression/value in F# 10.0 (`let x = {||}` compiles and evaluates to `{ }` of type `{| |}`). The 2019 discussion concluded it was a bug that `{||}` could be used as a value at all — but that was never fixed. As a result the asymmetry persists: it is a valid value but cannot appear in type position. Leaving the design decision (allow-as-type vs. disallow-as-value) to maintainers, but reporting the inconsistency for completeness. > [!NOTE] >

**Reproduction (FCS Symbol API)**

```fsharp
#r "/path/to/FSharp.Compiler.Service.dll"
open FSharp.Compiler.CodeAnalysis
open FSharp.Compiler.Text

let checker = FSharpChecker.Create()
let source = """
type T() =
    member x.Method() = ()
    member x.Prop = 42
"""
let filename = "test.fsx"
let opts, _ = checker.GetProjectOptionsFromScript(filename, SourceText.ofString source) |> Async.RunSynchronously
let _, check = checker.ParseAndCheckFileInProject(filename, 0, SourceText.ofString source, opts) |> Async.RunSynchronously

match check with
| FSharpCheckFileAnswer.Succeeded res ->
    for sym in res.GetAllUsesOfAllSymbolsInFile() do
        match sym.Symbol with
        | :? FSharp.Compiler.Symbols.FSharpMemberOrFunctionOrValue as mfv when mfv.DisplayName = "Method" ->
            printfn "IsMethod=%b, IsMember=%b" mfv.IsMethod mfv.IsMember
            // Output: IsMethod=false, IsMember=true   ← bug: IsMethod should be true
        | _ -> ()
| _ -> ()
```

**Root cause observation**: When `GetAllUsesOfAllSymbolsInFile` returns symbol uses, F# methods on types are wrapped as `V vref` (a `ValRef`) in the `FSharpMemberOrFunctionOrValue` discriminated union — not as `M minfo`. The `IsMethod` property only returns `true` for the `M _` case, missing the case where a method is represented as a `V vref` with `v.IsMember = true`.

The `IsMember` flag correctly returns `true` for methods (since `V v -> v.IsMember`), but `IsMethod` doesn't have a corresponding `V v -> v.IsMember && not v.IsProperty` path.

Same observation for `IsProperty`: auto-properties (`member x.Prop = 42`) also show `IsProperty=false` via this path, while explicit get/set properties show `IsProperty=true` only on the third occurrence (the property binding itself, not the accessor methods).

🤖 *This is an automated response from Repo Assist.*

Still present in **F# 10.0** (FSI 14.0.105.0, commit `632a0ab`, repo as of 2026-03-25).

The F# compiler does not report an error when a class inherits from a C# abstract class that declares `abstract override` on a virtual method (e.g. `ToString`), and the F# subclass fails to provide an implementation. The build succeeds with zero warnings and zero errors, but the program crashes at runtime with `System.TypeLoadException`.

**Exact repro**

C# library (`cslib.csproj`, targeting `net9.0`):

```csharp
namespace CSharpLib
{
    public abstract class AbstractClass
    {
        public abstract override string ToString();
    }
}
```

F# project (references cslib):

````fsharp
open CSharpLib

type T() =
    inherit AbstractClass()
// NOTE: no override of ToString

[(EntryPoint)]
let hello _ =
    let t = T()
    printfn "Hello %O" t
    0
```

**`dotnet build` output** — 0 warnings, 0 errors (bug: should report a missing implementation error)

**`dotnet run` output:**
```
Unhandled exception. System.TypeLoadException: Method 'ToString' in type 'T'
from assembly 'fsproj, ...' does not have an implementation.
````

The compiler correctly detects missing implementations for ordinary `abstract` F# members and C# abstract members, but the `abstract override` combination (re-abstracting a previously virtual method from a base class) is not handled. The fix likely needs to be in the member override resolution logic that checks whether all abstract members are covered.

🤖 *This is an automated response from Repo Assist.*

Still present in **F# 10.0** (FSI 14.0.105.0, commit `632a0ab`, repo as of 2026-03-25).

The parser treats `let x = e1 in e2` followed by `e3` on a new line as `let x = e1 in (e2; e3)` instead of `(let x = e1 in e2); e3`. This means `x` remains in scope in `e3` when it should not be, and `e3` is silently included in the let-body rather than following it in sequence.

**Exact repro (script file):**

````fsharp
// x should be unbound after the let...in... expression, but it is not
let result =
    let x = 5 in x + 1
    x   // expected: FS0039 (x not defined); actual: compiles, result = 5

printfn "result=%d" result  // prints 5 (x's value), not 6 (x+1)
```

**Output:**

```
/path/test.fsx(3,18): warning FS0020: The result of this expression has type 'int'
and is implicitly ignored. ...
result=5
````

The warning fires on `x + 1` at col 18 (inside the incorrectly-extended let body). `result` is `5` — the value of `x` — rather than `6` (`x + 1`) or a compile error. The correct parse would give `result = 6` if `x` is the last expression in the body, but under the correct grouping `(let x = 5 in x+1); x` the final `x` should be `FS0039`.

🤖 *This is an automated response from Repo Assist.*

This issue is **still present** in F# 10.0 / FSI 14.0.105.0 (commit `632a0ab`, 2026-03-25).

**Root cause identified** in `src/Compiler/Checking/NameResolution.fs`, function `ResolveCompletionsInType`:

The `allowObsolete` parameter is correctly applied to **methods** (line 4560: `not (MethInfoIsUnseen g m ty minfo allowObsolete)`) and **properties** (line 4519–4523: `not (PropInfoIsUnseen m allowObsolete x)`), but is **not applied** to:

- **IL fields** (`finfos`, lines 4480–4485) — no `allowObsolete` check
- **Events** (`einfos`, lines 4465–4471) — no `allowObsolete` check  
- **F# record/class fields** (`rfinfos`, lines 4451–4453) — no `allowObsolete` check

This is why the screenshot in the issue shows the obsolete **field** and **event** appearing in completion while the obsolete **method** and **property** do not — the former two skip the filter entirely.

**Fix**: Add `allowObsolete` filtering to `einfos`, `finfos`, and `rfinfos` in `ResolveCompletionsInType`. For example, `einfos` should filter by `EventInfoIsUnseen` (analogous to `PropInfoIsUnseen`/`MethInfoIsUnseen`), and `finfos` / `rfinfos` should check `CheckILFieldAttribsForUnseen` / the F# attribute helpers.

This issue is **still present** in F# 10.0 / FSI 14.0.105.0 (commit `632a0ab`, 2026-03-25).

**Verified repro**:
````fsharp
type T() =
    static member Instance = T()
    member _.Method(_: double) = ()
    member _.Method(_: int) = ()

T.Instance.Method("")
```
```
test.fsx(6,1): error FS0041: No overloads match for method 'Method'.
````

The error starts at column **1** (the `T` in `T.Instance.Method("")`) rather than at the position of `Method`.

**Root cause**: In `CheckExpressions.fs`, `TcMethodApplicationThen` (line 9918) builds:
```fsharp
let mWholeExpr = (m, args) ||> List.fold (fun m arg -> unionRanges m arg.Range)
```
where `m` is the range covering the object expression (`T.Instance`) too. This `mWholeExpr` is then passed as `mMethExpr` to `TcMethodApplication` and ultimately to `ResolveOverloadingForCall` (line 10402):
```fsharp
let result, errors = ResolveOverloadingForCall denv cenv.css mMethExpr ...
```
The `FailOverloading` function uses this range directly for the error, so the squiggly covers the entire call expression.

`mItem` (the range of just the method name `Method`) is available at the call site and would produce a more precise error. Changing line 10402 to use `mItem` instead of `mMethExpr` for the overload resolution failure range would fix this — though care is needed to check whether `mItem` is always a tighter range than `mMethExpr`.

let _, result = checker.ParseAndCheckFileInProject("test.fsx", 0, SourceText.ofString code, projOptions) |> Async.RunSynchronously

match result with

    for sym in checkResults.GetAllUsesOfAllSymbolsInFile() do
        match sym.Symbol with
        | :? FSharpMemberOrFunctionOrValue as mfv ->
            let enc = match mfv.DeclaringEntity with Some e -> e.DisplayName | None -> "(none)"
            printfn "MFV: %-12s IsProperty: %-5b CompiledName: %-12s Enclosing: %s"
                mfv.DisplayName mfv.IsProperty mfv.CompiledName enc
        | _ -> ()

MFV: Foo          IsProperty: true  CompiledName: get_Foo      Enclosing: Class1  ← property itself ✓

module Nested =

    let bar = 456   // ← previously compiled silently; now gives FS0010

```

Result with `dotnet fsi test.fsx` (F# 10.0 / strict indentation default):

```
test.fsx(8,5): error FS0010: Unexpected keyword 'let' or 'use' in interaction. Expected ';', ';;' or other token.

test.fsx(10,1): error FS0058: Unexpected syntax or possible incorrect indentation: this token is offside of context started at position (8:5). Try indenting this further.
````

With `--langversion:7.0` the FS0010 remains an error and FS0058 is emitted as a warning instead of an error.

The silent misbehavior described in the issue (code compiling without any indication of the context reset caused by `#load`) is no longer present. Strict indentation enforcement (introduced in F# 8) made the problematic indentation an error.

**Clean repro** showing the closure-specific nature of the issue:

C# library:
```csharp
public class Base
{
    protected string Field = "hello";
}
```

F# consumer:
````fsharp
type Inherited() =
    inherit Base()

    // ✅ Direct access compiles fine — Field IS accessible from inherited type
    member x.Hey1 = x.Field

    // ❌ Via local closure fails with FS1097
    member x.Hey2 =
        let f () = x.Field   // error FS1097
        f ()
```

Error:
```
error FS1097: The struct or class field 'Field' is not accessible from this code location
````

The field is accessible in direct member context (`Hey1` works), but the F# compiler rejects the same access when it appears inside a local nested function (`f`). The error message `FS1097` gives no indication that the closure context is the problem, making it hard to diagnose.

module Thing
let error =

````xml

```

FSC : error FS2014: A problem occurred writing the binary '...':

The closure type `f@3TTTTTD` encodes six tuple patterns; the `tupledArg` field collides during IL field table construction. This is a pure compiler codegen issue — the code is semantically correct and runs fine in FSI.

🤖 *This is an automated response from Repo Assist.*

I've traced the root cause and have a confirmed one-line fix for this bug.

**Root cause**: In `src/Compiler/CodeGen/IlxGen.fs`, the `GenAttribArg` function handles `Const.Zero` (the F# default for all value types, used when `[(Optional)]` is specified without `[(DefaultParameterValue)]`) for `System.Object`, `System.String`, and `System.Type` — but not for `System.Boolean`. It falls through to the catch-all `InternalError`.

**The fix** — add one case after line ~10219 in `IlxGen.fs`:

```fsharp
| Const.Zero when isobj -> ILAttribElem.Null
| Const.Zero when tynm = "System.Boolean" -> ILAttribElem.Bool false   // ← add this line
| Const.Zero when tynm = "System.String" -> ILAttribElem.String None
| Const.Zero when tynm = "System.Type" -> ILAttribElem.Type None
```

**Regression test** to add in `tests/FSharp.Compiler.ComponentTests/Conformance/BasicGrammarElements/CustomAttributes/Basic/`:

```fsharp
// OptionalBoolAttributeArg.fs
open System
open System.Runtime.InteropServices

type FooAttribute(foo: string, bar: bool) =
    inherit Attribute()
    new([(Optional)] bar) = FooAttribute("", bar)

[(Foo)]
type Bar() = class end
```

This should compile successfully without a `[(DefaultParameterValue)]` attribute. The fix applies equally to other primitive value types if they encounter the same pattern.

🤖 *This is an automated response from Repo Assist.*

This issue appears to be fixed in recent versions of the F# compiler. SRTP now correctly selects the more specific `DU.ToCaseName` overload over the generic `CaseName.ToCaseName` — the wrong overload is no longer committed.

**Verified with current compiler:**
```fsharp
type CaseName =
    static member ToCaseName<'a, 'b when 'a : null>(value: 'a) = failwith "delay"
    static member ToCaseName(value: 'a) = 
        let case, _ = Reflection.FSharpValue.GetUnionFields(value, typeof<'a>) in case.Name 
    static member inline Invoke(value: 'a) : string =
        let inline call (other: ^M, value: ^I) = ((^I or ^M) : (static member ToCaseName: ^I -> string) value)
        call (Unchecked.defaultof<_>, value)

type DU =
    | Case
    static member ToCaseName(x: DU) = 
        match x with Case -> "Customized"

printfn "%s" (CaseName.Invoke(Case))
// Output: Customized  ✅  (previously: "Case" — wrong overload was selected)
```

Note: The original repro used `Unchecked.defaultof` without explicit type argument; in modern F# this now requires `Unchecked.defaultof<_>` (FS0685).

Maintainer: please verify and close if confirmed.

This issue appears to have been fixed in F# 11.0.100 (SDK 10.0.300) by PR [#19340](https://github.com/dotnet/fsharp/pull/19340).

The fix ensures that `outref` parameters from IL interface methods (e.g., C# `out` parameters) are now correctly compiled as `out` in the F# implementation, making the generated IL consistent regardless of whether the interface is defined in F# or IL.

**Repro (before fix):** A C# interface with `out int i` parameter, implemented in F#, would compile the parameter as `ref int i` instead of `out int i`.

**After fix:** The parameter should now correctly be compiled as `out int i`.

This fix is included in the F# 11.0.100 / .NET SDK 10.0.300 release. If you're still seeing this behavior with that version or later, please reopen with a repro.

🤖 *This is an automated response from Repo Assist.*

This issue appears to be fixed by PR #19372 "Resolve ILVerify errors — adjust codegen (551 → 56 errors)", merged March 2026.

**Wave 2a** of that PR specifically addressed the root cause: the F# compiler was emitting `callvirt` for method calls on unboxed value types (e.g., `struct.GetHashCode()`, `struct.ToString()`). ECMA-335 requires `call` or `constrained callvirt` for value type methods — plain `callvirt` on an unboxed value type receiver is invalid and causes runtime crashes.

The fix guards `callvirt` emission with `boxity <> AsValue` in both `GenApp` and `GenILCall` in `IlxGen.fs`. The PR description explicitly mentions benchmarks for a `StructToString` case — the same scenario as this issue.

**To verify:** try running the repro code from the issue with the latest .NET SDK. The `let inline toString (x: ^a) = (^a : (member ToString : unit -> string) x)` call on `123` should now print `"123"` correctly instead of throwing `NullReferenceException`.

If confirmed fixed, this issue can be closed.

This issue appears to have been fixed by PR #19243 ("FSharp.Core 'query' related fixes"), merged on 2026-02-11.

The root cause — anonymous record expression trees using `Lambda.Invoke` instead of a direct constructor call when fields are in non-alphabetical order — was fixed in that PR. Specifically:

> Fixes #11131, Fixes #15648 — Anonymous record field ordering: Let-bindings now inlined instead of `Lambda.Invoke` pattern

Issue #11127 describes the same underlying bug: `{| z = c.n; b = c.n |}` (non-alphabetical order) generates an incorrect nested lambda `Invoke` in the expression tree, causing EF Core translation failures.

If you're able to verify the repro case now generates the correct constructor call expression tree, this issue could be closed.

🤖 *This is an automated response from Repo Assist.*

This issue appears to be fixed in current F# 10. Testing with the exact repro:

```fsharp
[(Measure)] type rad

[(Literal)]
let pi = 3.14(rad)

let a = pi

let f (a:float) = a

f a  // was: confusing "float != float" error; now: clear "float != float(rad)" error
```

**Current behavior (F# 10):** Correctly emits `error FS0001: This expression was expected to have type 'float' but here has type 'float(rad)'`. The unit of measure `(rad)` is preserved on the literal binding and clearly shown in the error message.

The original bug was that the `(rad)` unit was silently stripped from `[(Literal)]` bindings, and error messages showed both sides as just `float` (hiding the unit), making the error message confusing. Both issues are now resolved — units are correctly preserved and the error message shows the full type with the unit annotation.

🤖 *This is an automated response from Repo Assist.*

This issue reports that `FSharpEntity.IsByRef` returns `false` for the `byref`1` type (i.e. `byref<'T>`).

Based on analysis of the current codebase, this appears to be **fixed**. The `isByrefTyconRef` function in `TypedTreeOps.fs` now explicitly checks for all byref-family types:

```fsharp
let isByrefTyconRef (g: TcGlobals) (tcref: TyconRef) = 
    (g.byref_tcr.CanDeref && tyconRefEq g g.byref_tcr tcref) ||   // byref`1
    (g.byref2_tcr.CanDeref && tyconRefEq g g.byref2_tcr tcref) || // byref`2
    (g.inref_tcr.CanDeref && tyconRefEq g g.inref_tcr tcref) ||   // inref<>
    (g.outref_tcr.CanDeref && tyconRefEq g g.outref_tcr tcref) || // outref<>
    ...
```

Since `g.byref_tcr` is defined as `mk_MFCore_tcref fslibCcu "byref`1"`, calling `mfv.ReturnParameter.Type.TypeDefinition.IsByRef` on a method returning `byref<'T>` should now correctly return `true`.

Could a maintainer verify and close this issue if confirmed?

Since then, FSI has been updated to target `net9.0` (the current .NET runtime), which defines `NETSTANDARD2_1`. The `TaskBuilderBase.Using` overload for `IAsyncDisposable` is now compiled in:

```fsharp
// src/FSharp.Core/tasks.fs
#if NETSTANDARD2_1
member inline this.Using<'Resource, 'TOverall, 'T when 'Resource :> IAsyncDisposable | null>
    (resource: 'Resource, body: 'Resource -> TaskCode<'TOverall, 'T>)
    : TaskCode<'TOverall, 'T> = ...
#endif
```

If you can confirm this is now working in a recent .NET SDK (9.0+), please close this issue.

This issue appears to have been fixed by [PR #18784](https://github.com/dotnet/fsharp/pull/18784) (merged August 4, 2025), which specifically addressed the root cause described here.

**Root cause**: `Span(T)` and `ReadOnlySpan(T)` carry both `ObsoleteAttribute` (for older compiler compatibility) and `IsByRefLikeAttribute`. The completion system was treating any type with `ObsoleteAttribute` as obsolete, causing these types to be suppressed from IntelliSense results.

**Fix**: The PR extended both the F# attribute checking path (`CheckFSharpAttributesForObsolete`) and the IL/.NET attribute checking path (`CheckILAttributesForUnseen`) to exclude types marked with `IsByRefLikeAttribute` from being considered obsolete. This fix is available in .NET SDK 10.x.

A maintainer may want to verify this is working correctly and close the issue if so.

🤖 *This is an automated response from Repo Assist.*

This issue appears to be fixed in the current codebase. Based on code analysis of `src/FSharp.Core/prim-types.fs`:

The original bug was that `sbyte[]` was being compared as `byte[]` (unsigned), so `-1y < 0y` in array form returned `false` instead of `true`. In the current comparison code (`GenericCompare`), the only fast path for byte arrays is:

```fsharp
| :? (byte array) as arr1, (:? (byte array) as arr2) ->
    GenericComparisonByteArray arr1 arr2
```

Since `sbyte[]` and `byte[]` are distinct types in the CLR (value-type arrays are not covariant), a `sbyte[]` does **not** match `:? (byte array)`. Instead, sbyte arrays fall through to `GenericComparisonArbArrayWithComparer`, which compares elements via `IComparable`, correctly using signed semantics.

This aligns with a comment in the issue thread pointing to a fix in visualfsharp PR #5267 (June 2018), and dotnet/fsharp was subsequently created from visualfsharp with that fix included.

**Suggested repro to confirm closure:**
```fsharp
let a = -1y
let b = 0y
printfn "%b" ([|a|] < [|b|])  // Should print: true
```

If this now prints `true`, the issue can be closed.

🤖 *This is an automated response from Repo Assist.*

This issue appears to have been fixed in recent .NET SDK versions (tested with .NET 9.0.114).

**Verification (tested with `dotnet fsi` / .NET 9.0.114):**

```fsharp
type A = A of B
and B = B of A | C

// Case 1: non-recursive (original bug: a1 = B (A null))
let rec a1 = B (A b1)
and b1 = B (A c1)
and c1 = C

// Case 2: true recursive (original bug: a2 = A null, b2 = B (A null))
let rec a2 = A b2
and b2 = B a2
```

**Results:**
- Case 1: `a1 = B (A (B (A C)))` — no null, correctly initialized ✅
- `b1 = B (A C)` — correct ✅
- Case 2: `a2` and `b2` form a valid circular reference, no null fields ✅

Null checks confirm none of the fields are null. The issue as originally reported (fields being `null`) no longer reproduces. Maintainers may wish to verify this is fixed in the release notes and close the issue.
